title: Control of bio-regenerated granular activated carbon by spreadsheet modelling
abstract:
J. Chem. T echnol. Biotechnol. 1998, 71, 253È261
Control of Bio-regenerated Granular Activated
Carbon by Spreadsheet Modelling
Miklas Scholz* & Robert J. Martin
The University of Birmingham, School of Civil Engineering, Edgbaston, Birmingham B15 2TT, UK
(Received 19 March 1997; revised version received 19 October 1997; accepted 28 October 1998)
Abstract: The optimisation of water puriÐcation with biological activated carbon
(BAC) is described. Procedures are suggested to control bioÐlm growth and to
use bio-indicators to predict TOC (total organic carbon) and COD (chemical
oxygen demand) removal efficiencies. Empty bed contact time (EBCT) was a
major physical control parameter. Dissolved oxygen (DO), pH and nutrients of
the inÑuent were controlled according to the abundance of bacteria, protozoa
and rotifers. Numbers of micro-organisms in BAC beds were determined. Certain
genera of ciliated protozoa, representing healthy environmental conditions, were
employed as biological indicators for system performance during biological
regeneration of exhausted granular activated carbon (GAC). There was a strong
positive correlation between the abundance of some protozoa in the liquid phase
of the BAC bed and COD concentration in the effluent. Mathematical spread-
sheet models were constructed to estimate COD removal efficiency of BAC Ðlters
with di†erent loading rates, DO, pH, nutrient requirements and populations of
micro-organisms.
1998 SCI
(
J. Chem. T echnol. Biotechnol. 71, 253È261 (1998)
Key words: water puriÐcation; biological activated carbon; control of pH and
dissolved oxygen; empty bed contact time; spreadsheet modelling; protozoa
1
INTRODUCTION
The treatment of river and reservoir water with bio-
logical activated carbon (BAC) is increasing because a
greater understanding of the combined adsorption and
biological regeneration process has been gained.1h3 One
of the major problems concerning BAC Ðltration for
water puriÐcation is the need to control bioÐlm devel-
opment on the granules without frequent backwashing
that would destroy the natural bioÐlm.4 It is difficult to
arrest biological activity because of the high adsorption
potential
of
granular
activated
carbon
(GAC)
for
organic matter and the ease of bacterial colonisation on
the rough, porous GAC surface. Even a high dose of
chlorine (2 mg dm~3 h~1) may not be e†ective to steril-
ise the system.1,5
Frequent thermal regeneration of BAC is often not
necessary. Partial biological regeneration of GAC due
* To whom correspondence should be addressed.
Contract/grant sponsor: University of Birmingham.
to sorption and later metabolism of slowly biodegrad-
able carbon can be observed in practice. This leads to
an extended life of the carbon bed. BAC can further
protect some bacteria against the negative inÑuence of
toxic substances. Surges of easily adsorbable matter in
the feed can be damped. For small changes in concen-
tration even virtual elimination of organic matter is pos-
sible.2 The removal of organic and/or toxic substances
is higher on BAC in comparison with GAC. This is par-
ticularly evident with reference to river water.1
Scholz and Martin investigated the ecological equi-
librium on BAC and possible parameters to control
biomass growth.3,4 Good removal efficiencies of feed
(mainly glycerol), nitrate, TOC (total organic carbon)
and COD (chemical oxygen demand) were achieved.
These workers also showed that backwashing was
reduced to a minimum for a steady state biomass com-
position with limited growth of Ðlamentous bacteria.
Reductions of dissolved oxygen (DO), pH and redox
potential in the bed were low during their study but
enough bacteria and protozoa were maintained to
253
1998 SCI.
J. Chem. T echnol. Biotechnol. 0268-2575/98/$17.50.
Printed in Great Britain
(
 254
M. Scholz, R. J. Martin
establish a strong protozoaÈbacteriaÈfungi-equilibrium
by keeping pH values high and maintaining a balanced
nutrition availability. Furthermore, high DO levels
resulted in high numbers of crawling protozoa and roti-
fers (good removal efficiency indicators), and in high
bacteria levels leading to high numbers of ciliated
protozoa. Finally, Ñagellates were present in signiÐcant
numbers after the system set-up (exhaustion period
dominated mainly by physical and chemical adsorption
processes), toxic shocks and pH swings.
On the other hand, there are also disadvantages of
BAC Ðltration without pH and DO control. The exces-
sive growth of slime-producing bacteria on BAC causes
clogging due to mud-ball formation. This results in high
pressure losses so that backwashing has to be under-
taken more often. Furthermore, an increase in bacteria
may be observed in the effluent from a BAC Ðlter. This
would require a higher chlorine demand for post-
chlorination.2 In contrast, work undertaken by Donlan
and Yohe shows that there is a low risk with respect to
BAC Ðlter operation;6 potentially pathogenic bacteria
are reduced by higher micro-organisms including bacte-
rivorous protozoa if optimal growth conditions are
present.
This study assesses the control of bioÐlm growth on
BAC; parameters studied including EBCT, DO, pH and
various nutrients. Correlations between changes in the
population of micro-organisms in BAC beds and COD
outÑow concentrations were found, and subjected to
extensive statistical analysis. That analysis is the basis
for two mathematical spreadsheet models estimating
COD removal efficiencies achieved by BAC Ðlters.
2
MATERIALS AND METHODS
A BAC pilot plant with four columns (BAC 1, BAC 2,
BAC 3 and BAC 4) was established. Figure 1 shows a
simpliÐed Ñow chart of the BAC column system. The
e†ects of di†erent control and inÑow water quality
parameters on micro-organisms were of interest. The
following combinations were possible to investigate the
inÑuence of DO or EBCT on the composition of higher
micro-organisms, including protozoa in BAC beds:
Combination IÈBAC 1 and BAC 2 (di†erence in DO)
Combination IIÈBAC 3 and BAC 4 (di†erence in DO)
Combination IIIÈBAC 1 and BAC 3 (di†erence in
EBCT)
Combination IVÈBAC 2 and BAC 4 (di†erence in
EBCT)
All other parameters were kept constant. Table 1 indi-
cates the parameters examined and initial conditions for
the performance of the pressure upward Ñow system
and Table 2 gives an overview concerning the di†erent
nutrition levels in the inÑuent. High initial concentra-
tions of glycerol as a carbon source were used to
shorten the exhaustion period of GAC and to simulate
very weak wastewater or polluted river water.
With the exceptions of COD, total plate count tests
and protozoa counting, all analytical procedures were
performed
according
to
the
American
Standard
Methods for the Examination of W ater and W astewater.7
COD and total plate count tests with nutrient agar
were based on British Standard Methods.8 In the mea-
surement
of
COD
below
concentrations
of
100 mg dm~3, it was necessary to concentrate the
sample by a factor of B20 to make the analysis viable.
Protozoa and other micro-organisms were counted in
the inÑuent, effluent and at Ðve or more positions
in the carbon bed itself by using a counting chamber
and a bright Ðeld/phase-contrast microscope (Olympus
Vanox, Tokyo, Japan, 204899). Some protozoa were
deÐned according to their movement in the liquid phase
around
carbon
particles:
free
swimming,
crawling,
stalked and Ñagellated protozoa. This classiÐcation is

Parameters to control and monitor biomass growth
The aim of the study was to control and monitor
biomass growth to optimise COD removal efficiency of
organic carbon. Glycerol was used as the main carbon
source (Table 2). DO and pH control were applied to
increase the abundance of ciliated protozoa and to
decrease growth rates of Ðlamentous bacteria and fungi.
It had previously been found that numbers of ciliates
correlated with adequately treated plant effluent, and
that Ðlamentous micro-organisms were responsible for
Ðlter-clogging phenomena.4 Average DO reductions
(DOr) per week are expressed in Table 3 (for example:
inÑuent DO [ effluent DO). BAC 2 and BAC 4 were
subject to aeration if the DO level in the effluent

dropped below 4 mg dm~3 (the critical value of the

The control of bacteria, fungi and protozoa growth
rates by means of parameters including DO and pH
was successful. It follows that the number of viable bac-
teria and fungi in the liquid phase of the carbon bed
was relatively low (102È104 colonies cm~3). Measure-
ments with the respirometer conÐrmed the results of
total plate count tests. During the actual experiment,
large protozoa were present in high numbers (102È
showed maximum growth rates (5È10 day~1) directly
after the system set-up and after great pH swings.
Further analysis showed that high DO levels resulted in
high numbers of crawling and stalked protozoa as well
as rotifers (100È500 countings cm~3) for each group.
The numbers of these micro-organisms correlated with
good COD removal efficiencies (Table 4). Bacteria levels
were high if ciliates were present in high numbers
(B400 bacteria colonies per ciliate).
Eckenfelder has deÐned the trace nutrients required
for biological oxidation, and the Ðfth column of Table 2
summarises some of his Ðndings.10 Of the trace minerals
were present at high concentrations in the inÑow water

after the 8th of October. However, there was a lack of
iron on the BAC granules. Therefore, iron was added as
a trace mineral at the end of the experiment to investi-
gate the biological response to that mineral (Table 2).
Statistical analysis to show the signiÐcance of
Statistical analysis was necessary to compare the abun-
dance of certain micro-organisms and COD effluent
concentrations for di†erent environmental conditions.
Four BAC Ðlters with slight di†erences in EBCT and
DO concentration were in operation (Table 1). The
combinations (mentioned in Section 2) showed sta-
tistically highly signiÐcant di†erences from each other
with respect to a selection of eight micro-organism
groups that are listed in Table 4. The analysis was
The ecological diversity of micro-organisms in a BAC
bed indicates how favourable environmental conditions
are. It had previously been found that a high diversity is
beneÐcial for an ecological equilibrium on BAC.3 The
ShannonÈWeaver diversity index (H) takes into account
how evenly the total number of individuals in a sample
is apportioned between each species and was calculated
regarding micro-organism diversity for all Ðlters that
are described in Table 1.9 The diversity was low
(H \ 0É4) during the system set-up. The population
diversity increased to H \ 1É0 after the inÑow pH value
had been increased (*pH 1É1). If the EBCT was low
(8 min), the ShannonÈWeaver diversity index was high
(H \ 1É5) for both simulation stages. The general devel-
opment of H was di†erent for all BAC Ðlters after trace
mineral levels were increased and glycerol as a carbon
source was decreased by 7É07 mg dm~3 (Table 2). The
ShannonÈWeaver diversity index of BAC 1 decreased
initially to H \ 0É3 after B2 weeks and then increased
rapidly to 1É4 after having been constantly low for
another week. It increased gradually to 1É3 for BAC 2
and Ñuctuated greatly between 0É1 and 1É4 for BAC 3.
Small variations of the ShannonÈWeaver index for BAC
4 around 1É0 could be observed.
Another statistical tool can be selected to demon-
strate that high species diversity is also an indicator for
good COD removal efficiencies. The Spearman rank
correlation coefficient was applied to estimate the corre-
lation between the COD removal efficiency and the
organism population diversity for all four BAC Ðlters.
Because observations, measurements and countings
have to be converted to ranks, the Spearman rank
correlation coefficient is only used for ordinal scales.
Ordinal numbers may not indicate absolute quantities.
Counts as well as observations do not have to be dis-
tributed normally; they can be distribution free.9,11 The
COD removal efficiency a few weeks after nutrient level
control (Table 2) was introduced. A high diversity was
an indicator for good COD removal efficiencies. This
result is consistent with conservation theories that
emphasise the importance of a rich population diversity
as a necessity for an ecosystem that has reached an
It remains to be investigated whether COD removal
efficiencies of all Ðlters are signiÐcantly di†erent from
each other, possibly as a result of signiÐcant di†erences
in operational conditions. The G-test can be used to
show that COD removal efficiencies were not similar for
Ðlters with slight di†erences in EBCT and DO (Table 1).
zones) ] (average COD removal efficiencies) contin-
gency table for di†erent Ðlter combinations (see above).
The level of probability for “di†erence in proportionsÏ
concerning a combination can be estimated with the
G-test, which is similar to the well known chi-test. In
this case, the chi-test would probably be applied more
often in practice. However, the G-test is much easier to
conduct, more conservative and mathematically more
advantageous. There were large di†erences regarding all
combinations of BAC Ðlter COD removal efficiencies
on a day to day basis. The probability that data sets
concerning combination I (see above) are similar, was
O5% and the probability that all other combinations
are also similar was O1%. However, the absolute
average COD removal efficiency di†erence between all
combinations was low (O5%). A comparison of the
overall performance showed that a Ðlter with a low
EBCT and/or no DO control system was more suc-
cessful. Therefore, BAC 1 (EBCT: 8 min; no DO
The G test statistic was further used for a (Ðlter
parameter; “DO control on/o†Ï and EBCT) ] (average
micro-organism distribution) contingency table for dif-
ferent Ðlter combinations (see above). This contingency
table allows the investigation of association between the
frequencies of operational parameters (EBCT and DO)
and micro-organism distribution. The level of “di†erence
in proportionsÏ was statistically very highly signiÐcant
for all combinations, with a probability of O1% that
the proportions were similar. This reÑected the fact that
the di†erences of average numbers of micro-organisms
regarding the liquid phases of two BAC Ðlters were high
(approx. between 1000 and 6000). It was concluded that
it is statistically very highly signiÐcant that there was a
EBCT on one side and also between micro-organisms
and DO concentration on the other side.
The G-test indicated that COD removal efficiencies
were di†erent on a day to day basis for all Ðlter com-
binations. However, the KruskalÈWallis test does not
provide general support for this conclusion, as the two
tests have di†erent objectives. The KruskalÈWallis test
only compares medians of removal efficiencies for BAC
Ðlters and was carried out in this study to compare its
performance with the G-test. The simple non-parametric
KruskalÈWallis test statistic (K) for grouped average
COD removal efficiencies of all four columns was
applied to investigate possible di†erences regarding the
medians as a measure of central tendency within the
group of BAC Ðlters as a whole.9 The KruskalÈWallis
test statistic compared medians of unmatched samples
(+di†erent BAC Ðlters) and a K value of 0É23 was con-
sidered to be very low. In conclusion, there were no sta-
tistically signiÐcant di†erences regarding the medians.
Furthermore, the rank number distribution showed a
similar optical pattern for all BAC Ðlters but especially
for the combinations I and II (see above). The inÑuence
of EBCT concerning the rank distribution was small,
for example between 22/09/96 and 07/10/96.
The KruskalÈWallis test statistic was also applied for
total and grouped average numbers of micro-organisms
of all four columns to investigate possible di†erences
regarding the medians within the sum of BAC Ðlters. A
K value of 5É82 for total average numbers of micro-
organisms showed that there were no great di†erences
of the median variables with respect to micro-organisms
inhabiting BAC Ðlters. The only statistically signiÐcant
di†erences found within the rhizopoda group (except
actinopoda) were between the medians for those popu-
lations (K \ 9É15). This shows that the KruskalÈWallis
test is not appropriate to detect potential bio-indicators
[except rhizopoda (without actinopoda)].
The statistics reported above support theoretical evi-
dence for a signiÐcant relationship between the control
organisms. It is important also for practical purposes to
examine the strength of the correlation between COD
removal efficiency and the presence of certain micro-
organisms (Tables 4 and 5). Overall TOC removal effi-
ciencies were between 15 and 35%. COD removal
efficiencies per week are given in Fig. 2. Table 4 shows
the strength of correlation between mean COD concen-
trations (\18 mg dm~3) and the means of micro-
organism groups in the BAC bed (counts cm~3 liquid
phase of the carbon column). Furthermore, Table 5 is a
guide for BAC Ðlter operation managers to estimate
COD and TOC removal efficiencies with indicator
Two models were developed to give the BAC treatment
plant manager a practical tool for estimating COD
removal efficiencies. They are based on the statistical
analysis discussed in the previous section. The Ðrst
model, “BAC 2000Ï, estimated COD removal efficiencies
with the help of the following parameters: DO, EBCT,
pH, free swimming protozoa, crawling protozoa, Ñagel-
lates, rotifers and an estimated COD range. The model
was veriÐed with data obtained from BAC 2. There was
a strong correlation between the predictions obtained
with “BAC 2000Ï and real measurements (R2 \ 0É77).
The model is applicable for a wide range of GAC or
BAC operations in the water industry. However, it does
not refer to the level of conÐdence concerning the esti-
The second model, “BAC 2001Ï, estimated COD and
TOC removal efficiencies. It is based on a few biological

indicator genera and/or species and some physical and
chemical control parameter (DO, EBCT, pH, nutrients
and trace minerals). The operator can choose between
four operation stages. The Ðrst stage “system set-upÏ is
applicable for fresh, not exhausted BAC. The second
stage “increase of pHÏ is applicable for BAC containing
a natural bioÐlm. All other stages are dominated by
biological regeneration phenomena of the exhausted
GAC. The “BAC 2001Ï model is a highly empirical
model applicable mainly for BAC columns similar to
the system developed by the authors. Furthermore, it
calculates a conÐdence level where accuracy is subject
to the limits of protozoa and/or rotifer counting ranges
deÐned in the model manual. Table 6 compares the
main input and output features of both models with
each other. “BAC 2000Ï and “BAC 2001Ï are not com-
The aim of this study to control micro-organism growth
and to use protozoa as bio-indicators to predict COD
